museum.tagline: Famous public-domain photographs, run through real image-processing code in your browser.
museum.intro: A small museum of image processing. Pick a photograph, then run classic algorithms on it, brightness and contrast, median and harmonic filters, edge and line detection, histogram equalisation, morphology, and the Fourier domain, and see the result straight away. The algorithms are the actual C++ I wrote in 2019; nothing is sent anywhere, it all runs on your machine.
museum.pipelineExplainer: Each operation is the original C++ program, compiled to WebAssembly and run unchanged. When you apply one, the canvas pixels are written to a BMP in Emscripten's in-memory filesystem; the C++ reads that file, runs the operation, and writes a BMP back; the result is drawn to the canvas. The same code you'd run in a terminal, running in a browser tab.
museum.creditsNote: The curated gallery is drawn from public-domain collections, NASA, the Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information, the National Archives (Ansel Adams's National Park Service series), and the 1873 Wheeler Survey, via the Library of Congress, NARA and Wikimedia Commons.

exhibits.title: Earthrise
exhibits.collection: NASA (Apollo 8, Lunar and Planetary Institute / NASA Johnson Space Center photo archive)
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Public domain in the United States as a work created solely by NASA, an agency of the U.S. federal government. Under 17 U.S.C. § 105, works produced by U.S. government employees in the course of their official duties carry no copyright (PD-USGov / PD-USGov-NASA). NASA's media-usage policy further states that NASA imagery is not protected by copyright unless specifically noted, and no such note applies to AS08-14-2383.
exhibits.placard: On December 24, 1968, as Apollo 8 rounded the far side of the Moon on its fourth orbit, the gray lunar horizon suddenly gave way to a small blue Earth rising into the black. Astronaut Bill Anders grabbed a 70mm Hasselblad and color film and pressed the shutter. Catalogued NASA AS08-14-2383, the frame showed humanity its fragile home from 240,000 miles away. Often called the most influential environmental photograph ever taken, it helped ignite the modern ecological movement.
exhibits.altText: The blue-and-white Earth, half in shadow, rising above a barren gray, cratered lunar horizon against the black emptiness of space, photographed from Apollo 8 in lunar orbit.

exhibits.title: The Tetons and the Snake River
exhibits.collection: U.S. National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), Record Group 79, Records of the National Park Service; "Ansel Adams Photographs of National Parks and Monuments" (NARA ID 519904 / Local ID 79-AAG-1)
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Public domain as a work of the U.S. federal government. Adams made the image for the National Park Service / Department of the Interior mural project, so it was created as part of a U.S. Government employee/officer commission and carries no copyright under 17 U.S.C. § 105 (Commons template PD-USGov). Held by NARA with no usage restrictions.
exhibits.placard: In 1942, Ansel Adams aimed his large-format camera across Wyoming's Snake River as it coils toward the storm-lit ramparts of the Grand Tetons. The frame was made for a National Park Service mural project, halted by World War II yet never forgotten. Adams fused his Zone System craft with a conservationist's reverence, rendering water, forest, and granite in luminous tonal scale. The result became an icon of American wilderness photography, so emblematic that a copy now travels aboard NASA's Voyager Golden Record, carrying these mountains beyond the solar system.
exhibits.altText: Black-and-white landscape photograph of the snow-streaked Grand Teton mountain range under a dramatic cloud-filled sky, with the bright S-curve of the Snake River winding through a dark evergreen valley in the foreground.

exhibits.title: Trees with Snow on Branches, "Half Dome, Apple Orchard, Yosemite"
exhibits.collection: U.S. National Archives (NARA), Record Group 79, Records of the National Park Service; "Ansel Adams Photographs of National Parks and Monuments" (NARA ID 520018)
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Held by the U.S. National Archives and Records Administration in the National Park Service records (RG 79). NARA designates the item with no known copyright restrictions, and Wikimedia Commons hosts it under a Public Domain mark. The photographs in this Department of the Interior / National Park Service series were produced as part of Adams's federal mural commission and are treated as works of the U.S. Government, placing them in the public domain in the United States.
exhibits.placard: In April 1933, Ansel Adams turned his large-format lens on Yosemite Valley, framing the sheer granite face of Half Dome behind a bare apple orchard glazed in fresh snow. Adams later made these prints for the U.S. National Park Service as studies for a Department of the Interior mural. With near-white snow against black rock and skeletal branches, the picture distills his lifelong pursuit of tonal extremes, proof that wilderness, precisely seen, could become a national argument for preservation.
exhibits.altText: Black-and-white photograph of bare apple orchard trees coated in snow, with the steep granite cliff of Half Dome rising sharply in the background in Yosemite Valley.

exhibits.title: Migrant Mother, Nipomo, California
exhibits.collection: Library of Congress, Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information Photograph Collection
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Work created by an employee of the U.S. Farm Security Administration (a federal government photographic unit) as part of official duties. Under 17 U.S.C. § 105, works of the U.S. federal government are not eligible for copyright and are in the public domain. The original FSA negatives and prints carry no copyright; the Library of Congress lists no known restrictions on publication.
exhibits.placard: Dorothea Lange made this photograph in March 1936 at a frozen pea-pickers' camp in Nipomo, California, while documenting Depression hardship for the Farm Security Administration. In ten minutes she shot six frames of Florence Owens Thompson, a 32-year-old mother of seven, and chose this one: a weathered hand at her jaw, two children turned away, an infant in her lap. Published nationwide, it forced relief to the camp and became the defining image of American documentary photography, the human face of the Great Depression.
exhibits.altText: A weary mother in a worn jacket gazes into the distance with a worried expression, her hand against her cheek, an infant asleep in her lap and two children turning their faces against her shoulders, photographed in stark black and white.

exhibits.title: Ancient Ruins in the Cañon de Chelle
exhibits.collection: U.S. Library of Congress, Prints and Photographs Division (digital ID ppmsca.10055); made for the Wheeler Survey, U.S. War Department
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Public domain on two independent grounds: (1) the photograph was created in 1873 as part of O'Sullivan's official duties on the U.S. Army's Wheeler Survey, making it a work of the U.S. federal government and therefore not subject to copyright; and (2) it was published in 1873, far more than 95 years ago, placing it in the public domain in the United States (all works published before 1929 are PD). The Library of Congress lists no known copyright restrictions.
exhibits.placard: In 1873, Timothy O'Sullivan hauled a wet-plate camera into Arizona's Canyon de Chelly while photographing for the U.S. Army's Wheeler Survey. He aimed his lens at the sheer sandstone wall sheltering the Ancestral Puebloan cliff dwelling later called the White House, perching the masonry ruins fifty feet above the canyon floor. The towering, light-streaked cliff dwarfs the human work below. A masterpiece of frontier survey photography, it fused scientific record with the sublime-shaping how Americans first imagined the West.
exhibits.altText: A towering vertical sandstone cliff face streaked with dark mineral varnish fills the frame; tucked in a shadowed niche near its base sit small pale stone-and-adobe ruins of an Ancestral Puebloan cliff dwelling, with rubble and a sliver of canyon floor below.

exhibits.title: American Gothic, Washington, D.C.
exhibits.collection: Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Division, Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information (FSA/OWI) Collection (call no. LC-DIG-fsa-8b14845; digital ID fsa.8b14845)
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Public domain in the United States as a work of the U.S. federal government. Gordon Parks made this photograph in August 1942 as a staff photographer for the Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information, so under 17 U.S.C. Sec. 105 a work prepared by a federal employee in the course of official duties carries no copyright and is in the public domain from creation. The Library of Congress independently confirms the rights status: the item bears the advisory "No known restrictions," and LOC states that the FSA/OWI black-and-white negatives "are in the public domain and are free to use and reuse." Note: this PD basis applies ONLY to Parks's 1942 FSA/OWI-era images; his later Life magazine and commercial/editorial photographs remain under copyright and are NOT public domain.
exhibits.placard: In August 1942, Gordon Parks, newly hired by the Farm Security Administration, met Ella Watson, a Black charwoman who had cleaned federal offices for years on poverty wages. He posed her holding a broom and mop before the American flag, echoing Grant Wood's 1930 painting. Parks's first Washington picture turned a quiet portrait into a searing indictment of racial inequality at the heart of the wartime capital. It became his signature image and one of the most influential photographs of the twentieth century.
exhibits.altText: Black-and-white photograph of a Black woman in a dark dress standing before a large American flag, holding an upright broom in one hand and a mop beside her, looking directly at the camera.

exhibits.title: Old Faithful Geyser Erupting, Yellowstone National Park
exhibits.collection: U.S. National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), Record Group 79, "Ansel Adams Photographs of National Parks and Monuments," National Archives at College Park (NARA ID 519994)
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Public domain in the United States under 17 U.S.C. § 105: the photograph was produced as part of Adams's official duties under his 1941-1942 commission from the U.S. Department of the Interior / National Park Service for the planned Interior Building photo-murals. As a work prepared for the U.S. federal government it carries no copyright, and the NARA-held original is released with the Public Domain Mark 1.0. (This applies specifically to the NARA/NPS mural-project images, Adams's separately published Yellowstone prints held by museums remain under the Ansel Adams Trust's copyright.)
exhibits.placard: In 1941 the Interior Department hired Ansel Adams to photograph America's national parks for a set of building murals. Traveling that autumn into 1942, he caught Old Faithful mid-eruption, a luminous column of steam towering against a darkened Wyoming sky. World War II halted the project before completion, and the 226 surviving prints passed to the National Archives. Stark, vertical, and elemental, the image distills Adams's belief that wild land was a national treasure worth defending.
exhibits.altText: A tall, bright plume of steam and water from the erupting Old Faithful geyser rises against a dark sky, with the pale geyser cone and sparse trees at its base, in a vertical black-and-white photograph.

exhibits.title: Grand Canyon National Park, from Yavapai Point
exhibits.collection: U.S. National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), Record Group 79, Series AA: "Ansel Adams Photographs of National Parks and Monuments, 1941-1942," local identifier 79-AAF-19 (NAID 10583011). National Park Service Mural Project, Department of the Interior.
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Public domain in the United States as a work of the U.S. federal government. Adams made this frame on commission for the Department of the Interior / National Park Service as part of the Mural Project; NARA documents that these prints are property of the U.S. Government with no copyright restrictions. Under 17 U.S.C. §105, works prepared by a federal employee/agency in the course of official duties carry no copyright (Commons template PD-USGov-Interior-NPS). Note: this PD status applies specifically to the NARA-NPS 79-AA series and FSA/OWI-era Parks work, Adams's commercial and personal photographs (e.g., his Yosemite prints) remain under copyright and are NOT covered.
exhibits.placard: Standing at Yavapai Point on the South Rim in 1941, Ansel Adams aimed his large-format camera into the labyrinth of buttes and side canyons below. He made it for the National Park Service's Mural Project, a Depression-era commission to celebrate America's public lands. Light rakes across receding rock formations, compressing miles of geology into ridges of silver and shadow. The image distills Adams's lifelong argument: that wilderness, precisely seen, deserves protection. It survives, copyright-free, in the National Archives.
exhibits.altText: Black-and-white photograph looking out from Yavapai Point over the Grand Canyon, with sunlit rock formations and deep side canyons receding into shadow toward the far rim.

exhibits.title: Church, Taos Pueblo National Historic Landmark, New Mexico
exhibits.collection: U.S. National Archives (NARA), Record Group 79 (Records of the National Park Service), series "Ansel Adams Photographs of National Parks and Monuments," compiled 1941-1942; National Archives Identifier (NAID) 519983; local identifier 79-AA-Q01.
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Public domain as a work of the U.S. federal government (17 U.S.C. §105 / PD-USGov-Interior-NPS). In 1941-42 the Department of the Interior commissioned Adams to produce a photo mural documenting the National Parks and Monuments; the resulting negatives became U.S. government property and were never under private copyright. This is the crucial distinction: Adams's commercial Yosemite prints remain copyrighted by the Ansel Adams Publishing Rights Trust, but this NARA-NPS / Interior-commission series is genuinely PD. The Commons file carries the NPS-employee/US-federal-government public-domain template, and the original is held in NARA's Still Picture Records (College Park).
exhibits.placard: In 1942, the Department of the Interior commissioned Ansel Adams to photograph America's national parks and monuments for a planned mural project. At Taos Pueblo, New Mexico, he framed the front entrance of the mission church of San Gerónimo, a centuries-old adobe sanctuary fusing Spanish-Catholic and Pueblo traditions. Adams's exacting tonal control turns sunlit adobe and deep shadow into pure geometry. As a U.S. government commission, the image is public domain, freeing this monument of the Southwest for all.
exhibits.altText: Black-and-white photograph looking up at the front entrance of an adobe mission church at Taos Pueblo, with thick rounded earthen walls, a wooden cross atop the facade, and a bright Southwestern sky above.

exhibits.title: Mrs. Ella Watson, a Government Charwoman, with Three Grandchildren and Her Adopted Daughter
exhibits.collection: Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Division, Farm Security Administration/Office of War Information (FSA/OWI) Black-and-White Negatives
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Public domain as a work of the U.S. Government (PD-USGov): Gordon Parks made this photograph in August 1942 as a salaried staff photographer for the federal Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information. Works prepared by U.S. Government employees as part of their official duties carry no federal copyright (17 U.S.C. Sec. 105), and the Library of Congress lists no known rights restrictions on the FSA/OWI negatives. This is from Parks's 1942-43 federal employment, NOT his later copyrighted Life-magazine/commercial work.
exhibits.placard: Not the famous "American Gothic" broom-and-flag frame, this quieter portrait is Gordon Parks's family study of Ella Watson, a Washington charwoman who supported five children on $1,080 a year. Made in August 1942 for the federal FSA/OWI, it seats Watson in her cramped kitchen doorway cradling a grandchild, two more children nearby, while her adopted daughter appears as a reflection in the bureau mirror at right. Parks turned a government assignment into intimate witness to Black working-class life.
exhibits.altText: Black-and-white 1942 photograph: an African American woman, Ella Watson, sits in a small kitchen doorway holding a baby, with two young children near her; at right a dresser holds a framed portrait and a wall mirror reflects a teenage girl, her adopted daughter.

exhibits.title: Washington, D.C. Government charwoman (Ella Watson at work with mop)
exhibits.collection: Library of Congress, Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information (FSA/OWI) Black-and-White Negatives, LOT 156
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Created in August 1942 by Gordon Parks while a salaried federal photographer for the U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information. As a work prepared by an employee of the U.S. federal government as part of official duties, it is in the public domain (PD-USGov). The Library of Congress lists "No known restrictions" for the FSA/OWI black-and-white negatives, which it states are in the public domain and free to use and reuse. (This is the federal FSA/OWI work, NOT Parks's later copyrighted Life-magazine/commercial photography.)
exhibits.placard: In August 1942, FSA photographer Gordon Parks turned his lens on Ella Watson, a government charwoman who cleaned federal offices in wartime Washington, D.C. Here she stands amid shrouded desks, swivel chairs, and a waste bin at the close of her shift, mop in hand, the national flag pinned to the wall behind her. Unlike Parks's tightly framed "American Gothic" portrait, this wider scene situates Watson within the very workplace she maintained after hours, quietly indicting the gap between the flag's promise and a Black laborer's reality.
exhibits.altText: A Black woman in a polka-dot dress and round glasses stands holding a mop in a government office filled with desks, swivel chairs, and a waste bin, with a U.S. flag hanging on the wall behind her.
bestFor: Civil rights and labor history exhibits
Discussions of dignity of work and racial inequality in 1940s America
Companion piece contrasting Gordon Parks's 'American Gothic'

exhibits.title: Washington, D.C. Mrs. Ella Watson, a government charwoman, reading the Bible to her household
exhibits.collection: Library of Congress, Prints & Photographs Division, Farm Security Administration/Office of War Information (FSA/OWI) Black-and-White Negatives
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Created in August 1942 by Gordon Parks while he was a salaried photographer for the U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information, a federal agency. As a work prepared by an employee of the U.S. federal government as part of official duties, it carries no copyright and is in the public domain (PD-USGov). It is held in the Library of Congress FSA/OWI collection, which LOC publishes with no known copyright restrictions. This is distinct from Parks's later Life-magazine and commercial work, which IS copyrighted.
exhibits.placard: In the summer of 1942, FSA photographer Gordon Parks spent weeks with Ella Watson, a government charwoman, documenting her life beyond her famous "American Gothic" portrait. Here, in her apartment at 1433 11th Street NW, Washington, D.C., Watson reads the Bible to her household, her adopted daughter and grandchildren drawn close around her. Parks insisted on naming Watson and her family, resisting the FSA's tendency toward anonymous "types" and asserting their full human dignity.
exhibits.altText: Black-and-white photograph of an older Black woman, Ella Watson, seated in a sparsely furnished room reading from an open Bible, with her adopted daughter and several grandchildren gathered attentively around her in their Washington, D.C. apartment, 1942.

exhibits.title: Anacostia, D.C. Frederick Douglass housing project. A dance group
exhibits.collection: U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information (FSA/OWI) Photograph Collection, Library of Congress Prints & Photographs Division
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Created by Gordon Parks in July 1942 as a salaried federal photographer for the FSA/OWI, making it a work of the U.S. Government and in the public domain (PD-USGov). The Library of Congress lists "no known restrictions on publication" for the FSA/OWI black-and-white negatives. This is from his 1942-43 federal employment, not his later copyrighted Life-magazine/commercial work.
exhibits.placard: In July 1942, while employed by the Farm Security Administration, Gordon Parks documented daily life at Anacostia's Frederick Douglass housing project, a community for Black residents in wartime Washington, D.C. Here a group of children practices in a dancing class, their poised arms and bare feet caught mid-movement. Made during the same federal assignment that produced his famous "American Gothic," this quieter image reveals Parks's tender eye for ordinary moments of grace, dignity, and joy in everyday Black life.
exhibits.altText: Black-and-white 1942 photograph of a group of young Black children in a dance class at the Frederick Douglass housing project in Anacostia, Washington, D.C., posed with arms extended.
bestFor: civil rights and Black history exhibits
World War II home-front documentary photography
history of public housing and urban community life

exhibits.title: Daytona Beach, Florida. Bethune-Cookman College. Students using microscopes
exhibits.collection: Library of Congress, Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information (FSA/OWI) Black-and-White Negatives
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Created in February 1943 by Gordon Parks while employed as a staff photographer for the U.S. Office of War Information (domestic photographic unit, formerly FSA). As a work prepared by an employee of the U.S. federal government as part of official duties, it carries no copyright and is in the public domain in the United States (PD-USGov). It is held by the Library of Congress, which states the FSA/OWI negatives collection has no known copyright restrictions. This is the photographer's 1942-43 federal employment, not his later copyrighted Life-magazine/commercial work.
exhibits.placard: In February 1943, on assignment for the U.S. Office of War Information, Gordon Parks documented daily life at Bethune-Cookman College, a historically Black institution founded by educator Mary McLeod Bethune in Daytona Beach, Florida. Here students bend over microscopes in a science class, an image that quietly countered wartime racial stereotypes by picturing Black Americans as scholars and scientists. Parks framed education as a form of self-determination and national contribution, a recurring theme in his federal photography.
exhibits.altText: Black-and-white photograph of several young Black students seated at a laboratory table, leaning over and looking into microscopes in a science classroom at Bethune-Cookman College, 1943.
bestFor: World War II home-front documentary photography
Gordon Parks and FSA/OWI photography retrospectives
African American achievement and STEM education themes

exhibits.title: Operating a hand drill at Vultee-Nashville, woman working on a "Vengeance" dive bomber, Tennessee
exhibits.collection: U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information Color Photographs, Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division (LC-DIG-fsac-1a35371)
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Created by Alfred T. Palmer as a staff photographer for the U.S. Office of War Information, a federal government agency. As a work prepared by an officer or employee of the U.S. Government as part of official duties, it has no copyright protection under 17 U.S.C. § 105 and is in the public domain (PD-USGov). The Library of Congress lists no known restrictions on publication. On Commons it carries the PD US FSA/OWI template.
exhibits.placard: This vivid 1943 Kodachrome by Office of War Information photographer Alfred T. Palmer shows a real-life "Rosie the Riveter" at the Vultee Aircraft plant in Nashville, Tennessee, drilling a horizontal stabilizer for an A-31 "Vengeance" dive bomber. Her scarlet polka-dot bandana, painted nails, and mirrored reflection in the polished aircraft skin made it an enduring emblem of women's wartime industrial labor and a centerpiece of America's "arsenal of democracy" propaganda imagery.
exhibits.altText: A Black woman in a red polka-dot bandana and dark navy coverall operates a silver hand drill against the dark polished surface of an aircraft stabilizer, her face and red bandana mirrored in the metal.

exhibits.title: Mike Evans, a welder, at the rip tracks at Proviso yard of the Chicago and North Western Railroad, Chicago, Illinois
exhibits.collection: U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information Color Photographs, Library of Congress (FSA/OWI, FSAC), Washington, D.C.
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Public domain as a work of the U.S. Federal Government (PD-USGov): created by Jack Delano as an employee of the U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information domestic photographic units, in the course of official duties. Works of U.S. Government employees are not subject to copyright (17 U.S.C. 105), and the Library of Congress lists no known restrictions on publication.
exhibits.placard: Shot on 4x5 Kodachrome in April 1943, this portrait belongs to Jack Delano's wartime survey of American railroading for the Office of War Information. At the Chicago and North Western's vast Proviso yard, welder Mike Evans pauses amid the rip tracks where damaged freight cars were repaired. Delano's saturated color, then a costly novelty, dignifies ordinary labor sustaining the home-front war effort, capturing the soot, steel, and human face of 1940s industry.
exhibits.altText: A color photograph of a railroad welder, Mike Evans, standing at the repair tracks of a 1940s freight yard, wearing work clothes amid rail equipment.

exhibits.title: Jim Norris, homesteader, Pie Town, New Mexico
exhibits.collection: Library of Congress, Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information Color Photographs (FSA/OWI Kodachrome collection)
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Work created by Russell Lee as an employee of the U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information; as a work of the United States federal government it carries no copyright (PD-USGov). The Library of Congress lists the FSA/OWI Color Photographs as having no known copyright restrictions and free to use and reuse.
exhibits.placard: In the autumn of 1940, Russell Lee carried rare Kodachrome film to Pie Town, New Mexico, a hardscrabble homesteading community on the high desert. Here homesteader Jim Norris squints beneath a worn cowboy hat against a brilliant blue sky, every sun-creased line of his face rendered in then-revolutionary color. Made for the Farm Security Administration, the image trades Depression-era gray for vivid, dignified portraiture of ordinary rural Americans building lives on the frontier.
exhibits.altText: Color photograph of a weathered older man in a worn brown cowboy hat and collared shirt, squinting in bright sunlight against a clear blue sky.
bestFor: Great Depression and New Deal history

exhibits.title: A Crossroads Store, Bar, "Juke Joint," and Gas Station, Melrose, Louisiana
exhibits.collection: U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information Color Photographs, Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division (digital ID fsac.1a34361)
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Created in June 1940 by Marion Post Wolcott as a salaried photographer of the U.S. Farm Security Administration in the course of her official federal duties. As a work prepared by an employee of the United States federal government, it carries no copyright and is in the public domain in the United States (PD-USGov). It is held in the Library of Congress FSA/OWI Color Photographs collection, which the Library states has no known copyright restrictions.
exhibits.placard: Among the rarest of Depression-era records are the roughly 1,600 color frames the Farm Security Administration shot on then-new Kodachrome film. Here Marion Post Wolcott, the agency's first full-time woman photographer, trains that vivid palette on a weathered crossroads store, bar, "juke joint," and gas station in the cotton country near Melrose, Louisiana. The saturated reds and ochres lend a startling immediacy to a rural Southern scene more often seen in austere black and white.
exhibits.altText: Color photograph of a weathered wooden crossroads store, bar, juke joint, and gas station with hand-painted signs and a fuel pump, set in the flat cotton-plantation country near Melrose, Louisiana, in 1940.

exhibits.title: Seed and Feed Store, Lincoln, Nebraska
exhibits.collection: U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information Color Photographs, Library of Congress (LC-DIG-fsac-1a34282)
exhibits.publicDomainReason: Created by John Vachon as an employee of the U.S. Farm Security Administration / Office of War Information, a U.S. federal government agency. As an official work of the United States Government, it carries no copyright (PD-USGov) and is in the public domain worldwide. The Library of Congress lists no known copyright restrictions for the FSA/OWI color collection.
exhibits.placard: John Vachon shot this vivid Kodachrome on a 1942 assignment for the Farm Security Administration, part of the legendary color survey of wartime America. A modest Lincoln, Nebraska seed and feed store fills the frame, its windows crowded with hand-lettered signs advertising "Northern Grown Seed Potatoes." Where the FSA's famous black-and-white work documented hardship, these rare color frames render Main Street commerce in saturated reds, blues, and golds, preserving the everyday texture of small-town life on the home front.
exhibits.altText: Color photograph of a small-town storefront in Lincoln, Nebraska, 1942: a seed and feed store with large display windows covered in hand-painted signs, including one reading "Northern Grown Seed Potatoes."

exhibits.title: Hong Kong Stairway
exhibits.collection: Fan Ho · Hong Kong, 1950s-60s
exhibits.publicDomainReason: NOT public domain. Fan Ho (1931-2016) is a modern copyrighted photographer; his works remain under copyright and are reproduced here from a gallery source. This exhibit deviates from the museum's all-public-domain collection basis, include only with the rights holder's permission or for private, non-public study.
exhibits.placard: Fan Ho (1931-2016) shot Hong Kong from the 1950s with a Rolleiflex, working its markets, alleys and stairwells. Here a woman in a floral cheongsam comes down a grand staircase through shafts of hazy, raking light, other figures scattered on the steps beneath signs reading UP and DOWN. He waited on smoke, mist and backlight to turn ordinary public space into pure geometry and atmosphere.
exhibits.altText: A woman in a floral cheongsam descends a grand staircase through shafts of hazy backlight, other figures on the steps, signs reading UP and DOWN.

exhibits.title: Approaching Shadow (1954)
exhibits.collection: Fan Ho · Hong Kong, 1954
exhibits.publicDomainReason: NOT public domain. Fan Ho (1931-2016) is a modern copyrighted photographer; his works remain under copyright and are reproduced here from a gallery source. This exhibit deviates from the museum's all-public-domain collection basis, include only with the rights holder's permission or for private, non-public study.
exhibits.placard: Fan Ho's best-known photograph. His cousin stands against a bright textured wall while a hard diagonal shadow advances across it. Fan Ho strengthened that shadow himself in the darkroom, turning a plain wall into an image about youth and time running out. Made in 1954, when he was in his early twenties; work like this won him more than 280 international awards.
exhibits.altText: A young woman stands at the foot of a bright wall as a large diagonal shadow advances toward her.
